title: Hump-moulded plate in air-dry clay
subtitle: A shallow serving plate shaped over a dinner plate mould
excerpt: A hump-moulded plate is formed by draping a clay slab over an upturned dinner plate. The clay takes the plate's curvature as it stiffens and releases cleanly when leather-hard. No kiln or wheel is needed. The finished plate is decorative; air-dry clay is not food-safe. Use it to display bread, fruit, or ornaments.
sourceNotes: Hump-mould technique. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A convex surface over which a clay slab is draped to take the mould's outward curve. Used for shallow dishes and plates.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape and releases cleanly from the mould.

content.text: Draping a clay slab over an upturned dinner plate produces a shallow serving-plate form without a wheel or kiln. The technique is called a
hump mould

because the clay is draped over the outside of a convex mould rather than pressed into a concave one. Air-dry clay is not food-safe, so treat this as a decorative plate for bread, fruit in a bowl, or small objects.

attrs.title: Not food-safe
attrs.body: Air-dry clay is a decorative material. Do not use the plate for direct contact with food, damp items, or as a serving surface for anything eaten from the plate. A lining cloth or a piece of baking paper keeps the surface clean for bread display.

content.text: What you need

items.name: Dinner plate, smooth ceramic, 26-28 cm diameter (the mould)
Pottery rolling pin and 6 mm guide rails

content.text: Making the plate
Step 1: Prepare the mould
Turn the dinner plate upside down and cover the back surface with a smooth layer of cotton cloth or cling film. Smooth out all folds.
Step 2: Roll a slab wider than the plate

Roll a slab 6 mm thick and at least 5 cm wider than the plate in all directions. For a 27-cm plate, roll a slab at least 32 cm across. Fold in half, carry to the mould, and unfold over the mould surface from the centre outward. Smooth with a damp sponge.
Step 3: Add decoration to the surface

While the clay is still soft, press decorative elements into the clay surface facing upward: leaves, lace, stamps, or sgraffito-carved lines done at the later leather-hard stage. Large leaf impressions on the plate surface look particularly well on a pale air-dry clay body.
Step 4: Trim and release

Trim the edge with the fettling knife to the diameter you want the finished plate: a trim at the plate's rim line produces a plate the same size as the mould; a shorter trim makes a smaller plate. Allow to reach
leather-hard
on the mould, around two to four hours. The plate will pull away from the mould surface slightly. Lift the cloth at one edge to release.
Step 5: Finish the edge and dry
Turn the plate right-side-up and place it on a flat board. Smooth the rim with a wet finger or damp sponge. Allow to dry to bone-dry over one to two days. Seal with two coats of PVA diluted 1:1.
Variations
Add a slightly raised rim by pressing a thin coil around the edge at leather-hard and blending it in.
Use a small side plate as the mould for a bread-and-butter dish scale.
Common problems

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Issues when making the hump-moulded plate.

items.symptom: Plate warps as it dries right-side-up
items.cause: The bottom surface dried faster than the top once turned over.
items.fix: Dry the plate on the mould until it is nearly bone-dry before removing. Or place a flat board on top of the plate as it dries to press it flat.

items.symptom: Slab tears in the centre when draped
items.cause: Slab was too thin or dried too long before draping.
items.fix: Use a minimum 6 mm slab and drape while it is still soft off the rolling board.
